mnemonics.title: MVCC Version Visibility Rule
mnemonics.content: READ IF: version_commit_time < transaction_start_time AND version_not_deleted

mnemonics.title: Timestamp Order Rules
mnemonics.content: T/O Rules: READ-OK if TS(T) > WT(X), WRITE-OK if TS(T) > RT(X) and TS(T) > WT(X)

mnemonics.title: MVCC Benefits
mnemonics.content: NO-BLOCK: Readers never wait for writers, writers never wait for readers

acronyms.usage: Advanced concurrency control maintaining multiple data versions simultaneously
Ensures transaction logs written before data modifications for durability
Algorithm making snapshot isolation serializable by detecting conflicts
Concurrency control using transaction timestamps for serialization order
Process of removing obsolete data versions no longer needed

visualTips.visualization: Draw a linked list with each node representing a version. Latest version at head, arrows pointing to older versions. Show commit timestamps on each node.
Use a timeline showing transaction start times and commit times. Shade the 'visible window' for each transaction from time 0 to its start time.
Create a table with columns: Transaction ID, Status (Active/Committed/Aborted), Start Timestamp. Show how it determines version visibility.
Draw dependency graphs showing transactions as nodes. Mark edges for read-write conflicts. Identify dangerous cycles.

analogies.analogy: Think of version chains like a document tracking system where every edit creates a new file rather than overwriting, allowing anyone to view old versions while new edits happen.
Like taking a photograph of a busy scene - the photo captures the moment you pressed the shutter, regardless of changes happening after.
Like cleaning out old files in an archive - keeping only what's needed for current work while safely discarding outdated records.
Like a queue at a ticket counter where everyone gets a numbered ticket - service is based on arrival order (timestamp), ensuring fairness.
